message.content:
The wind holds its breath in the pine-scented air,
A hush falls heavy on the valley’s rim,
As if the world has paused, suspended there,
To watch the sky dissolve into a hymn.

Above the ridge, the clouds begin to fray,
Unspooling threads of silk and silver dust,
The first faint flakes drift softly, far away,
Like ghosts of summer memories we trust.

They land upon the roof of Miller’s shed,
A white kiss on the shingles, old and worn,
Where shadows stretch, then shrink, then softly spread,
And silence claims the dusk, the day is born.

Down in the square, the cobblestones grow pale,
Beneath the lantern’s amber, flickering glow,
The baker’s window holds a golden veil,
While outside, time moves slow, and soft, and low.

The church bell tolls, a muffled, distant sound,
Drowned by the whisper of the falling snow,
It wraps the steeple in a shroud profound,
And turns the river’s rush to quiet flow.

No footstep breaks the crust that forms so thin,
No voice disturbs the peace of evening’s grace,
The village sleeps beneath the sky’s thin skin,
A blank page waiting for a fresh embrace.

The chimneys puff their smoke in lazy rings,
A gray contrast against the pure white sheet,
The world forgets the clamor, all the stings,
And finds a cold and crystalline retreat.

So let it fall, this first and fragile gift,
To cover up the dirt, the stone, the street,
And in this silence, pure and deeply rifted,
The mountain and the village meet.

For now they are one entity, one breath,
